column1: You are on a flight with no internet. You need to commit 3 changes and read 50 commits of history. Can Git do this — and why?
column2: Yes — completely. Git stores every commit, every file version, and all history inside .git/ on your local machine. Internet is only required to push to or pull from a remote like GitHub. Both operations are fully local.

column1: A colleague says they pushed code to Git. What is imprecise about this statement?
column2: You push to a remote repository (GitHub / GitLab / origin) — not to Git itself. Git is the version control tool running locally. The correct statement: pushed to GitHub (or pushed to origin).

column1: Git uses content-addressed storage. You have 10 files with identical contents. How many objects will Git store for those 10 files?
column2: One. Git computes a SHA-1 hash of each object's contents. Same content → same hash → same storage key → one stored object. This is called deduplication and is automatic. (git add is covered in Demo 04 — this card tests the storage concept only.)

column1: What does content-addressed storage mean in Git?
column2: Objects are identified by what they contain, not where they are stored. Git computes SHA-1 hash of each object's contents and uses that hash as the storage key. Move or rename a file — the hash and stored object do not change.

column1: Name the three Git config scopes in order from lowest to highest precedence.
column2: system (lowest) < global < local (highest). Local always wins. global overrides system. Most specific scope takes priority.

column1: You use personal@gmail.com globally but need work@corp.com for one project. What is the exact command to set the override?
column2: cd into the project, then: git config --local user.email work@corp.com — this writes to .git/config inside the repo and overrides the global value only for that repository.

column1: git commit opens VS Code but creates an empty commit before you finish writing. What is wrong and what is the fix?
column2: core.editor is set to 'code' without --wait. Without --wait, Git continues immediately rather than waiting for you to close the file. Fix: git config --global core.editor 'code --wait'

column1: What is core.editor and when does Git open it during a git commit?
column2: core.editor is the text editor Git opens when you run git commit without the -m flag. Git writes a temporary file (COMMIT_EDITMSG), opens it in the editor, waits for you to save and close, then reads the message. Other uses (rebase, merge) are covered in later demos.

column1: Your commits appear as unknown user on GitHub. user.name and user.email are set locally. What is the most likely cause?
column2: The email in git config does not match any email on your GitHub account. GitHub links commits by matching the commit author email against all emails (primary + secondary) on your account. Add the local email to GitHub under Settings → Emails.

column1: What does core.pager do, and what does an empty value (core.pager=) mean?
column2: core.pager sets the program Git uses to page long output (default: less). An empty value disables paging — output flows directly to the terminal without stopping. Set with: git config --global core.pager '' to disable.

column1: What command shows every active Git config value AND the file each value comes from?
column2: git config --list --show-origin — prints source file path alongside each key=value pair. Essential for debugging when the same key appears in multiple scopes.

column1: What is the difference between a local repository, a remote repository, and a bare repository?
column2: Local repo: the .git/ folder on your machine — you commit and branch here. Remote repo: a copy hosted elsewhere (GitHub) — you push to and pull from it. Bare repo: a repo with no working tree — just the .git/ contents — used on servers; you never edit files directly in it.
